User.title: User
User.description: Represents a user registered in the Blauberia application, storing their unique identity and essential profile information.

id.description: Unique identifier for the User entity.

username.description: A unique username chosen by the user during registration. Must not contain emojis.

agreedToTerms.description: Indicates whether the user has agreed to the terms of service.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the user account was created.

definition.description: Stores individual user profiles, where the document ID (`{userId}`) matches the Firebase Authentication UID. Access is strictly restricted to the owner, ensuring private data. Authorization independence is achieved as the owner's UID is part of the path.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user, matching their Firebase Authentication UID.

firestore.reasoning:
The recommended Firestore structure for Blauberia prioritizes secure, scalable, and debuggable user data management. We will use a single top-level collection, `/users`, where each document's ID directly corresponds to the Firebase Authentication UID of the user. This path-based ownership model is fundamental to achieving robust security and authorization independence.

**Authorization Independence:** By making the document ID `{userId}` match the `request.auth.uid`, each user's profile is inherently authorized for access only by that specific user. This eliminates the need for any `get()` operations in security rules to check parent documents or external data for authorization, thereby supporting atomic operations and significantly simplifying rule debugging. The authorization logic becomes `allow read, write: if request.auth.uid == userId;`, which is clear, concise, and highly performant.

**QAPs (Query as a Principal):** For this `User` profile collection, the primary way a user accesses their own data is by directly referencing their document (`/users/{request.auth.uid}`). Direct `list` operations on the `/users` collection for unauthorized users will be explicitly denied in security rules, upholding the QAP principle. If there were a future need for a public user directory, a separate collection (e.g., `/public_profiles`) with only public user attributes would be created, ensuring distinct security postures and preventing unintended data exposure. This structural segregation ensures that `list` queries are inherently secure by matching the security requirements of the collection.
